question: Rev 18:4 'come out of her my people.' God's people inside Babylon. OT come-out tradition (Abraham, Exodus, literal Babylon). 2 Cor 6:14-18. Hina-me purpose clause. John 10:16. Fall-then-call sequence.

topics.name: BABYLON
topics.entry_text: Built by Nimrod GEN 10:10. In the land of Shinar GEN 10:10; 11:2. Tower of GEN 11:1-9. Capital of the kingdom of Babylon DAN 4:30. Gates of ISA 45:1,2; JER 51:58. Walled JER 51:44,58. Splendor of ISA 14:4. FIGURATIVE REV 14:8; 16:19; 17; 18. Prophecies concerning PSA 87:4; 137:8,9; ISA 13; 14:4-26; 21:1-10; 46:1,2; 47; 48:14,20; JER 21:4-10; 25:12-14; 50; 51; DAN 2:21-38; 4:10-26; 5:25-29; 7; HAB 1:5-11; ZEC 2:7-9.

topics.name: APOSTASY
topics.entry_text: Described DEU 13:13; HEB 3:12. Caused by persecution MAT 24:9,10; LUK 8:13. Caused by worldliness 2TI 4:10. Guilt and punishment of ZEP 1:4-6; HEB 10:25-31,39; 2PE 2:17,20-22. Shall abound in the latter days MAT 24:12; 2TH 2:3; 1TI 4:1-3. UNCLASSIFIED SCRIPTURES: DEU 32:15; 1CH 28:9; ISA 1:28; 65:11-16; JER 17:5,6; EZK 3:20; 18:24,26; 33:12,13,18; MAT 13:20,21; 24:10,12; JHN 15:6; ACT 7:39-43; 1CO 9:27; 2TH 2:3,11,12; 1TI 4:1,2; HEB 6:4-8; 10:26-29; 2PE 2:1,15,17,20-22; 3:17.

topics.name: SHEEP
topics.entry_text: 1CH 21:17; PSA 74:1; JER 13:20. Of backsliders JER 50:6. Of lost sinners MAT 9:36; 10:6. Of the righteous JER 50:17; EZK 34; MAT 26:31; MRK 14:27; JHN 10:1-16. Of the defenselessness of servants of God MAT 10:16. Parable of the lost MAT 18:11-13; LUK 15:4-7.

topics.name: ABRAHAM
topics.entry_text: Divine call of GEN 12:1-3; JOS 24:3; NEH 9:7; ISA 51:2; ACT 7:2,3; HEB 11:8. Lives in Ur, removes to Haran GEN 11:31; NEH 9:7; ACT 7:4. Canaan given to GEN 12:1,7; 15:7-21. Faith of GEN 15:6; ROM 4:1-22; GAL 3:6-9; HEB 11:8-10,17-19; JAS 2:21-24.

topics.name: CHURCH
topics.entry_text: Called FOLD OF CHRIST JHN 10:16. FLOCK OF GOD EZK 34:15; 1PE 5:2. BODY OF CHRIST EPH 1:22,23; COL 1:24. BRIDE OF CHRIST REV 21:9. TEMPLE OF THE LIVING GOD 2CO 6:16. One fold (flock) and one shepherd JHN 10:16.

verses.heading: Expanded fall announcement — demon-dwelling imagery preceding the come-out call
verses.kjv_text: And after these things I saw another angel come down from heaven, having great power; and the earth was lightened with his glory. And he cried mightily with a strong voice, saying, Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen, and is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird. For all nations have drunk of the wine of the wrath of her fornication, and the kings of the earth have committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth are waxed rich through the abundance of her delicacies.
verses.context: Rev 18 opens with an angel of great authority repeating the second angel's message of 14:8 with expanded detail. The egeneto (is become) marks progressive deterioration. Demon-dwelling imagery absent from 14:8 is now added.
verses.notes: Expansion from Rev 14:8 to 18:2 confirms Bohr's claim: demon-dwelling imagery is ADDED in 18:2 that was absent in 14:8. The egeneto verb (V-2ADI-3S, aorist middle indicative of ginomai) marks transition — Babylon HAS BECOME this. The doubled epesen epesen is prophetic aorist (V-2AAI-3S) as established in 3am-12.

verses.heading: CRUX TEXT — 'Come out of her, my people' with dual hina-me purpose clauses
verses.kjv_text: And I heard another voice from heaven, saying, Come out of her, my people, that ye be not partakers of her sins, and that ye receive not of her plagues. For her sins have reached unto heaven, and God hath remembered her iniquities.
verses.context: Another voice from heaven (allon phonen) — distinguished from the angel of 18:1. The possessive 'my people' (ho laos mou) is a covenant designation. The dual hina-me clauses give the PURPOSE of the command: (1) not sharing sins, (2) not receiving plagues.
verses.notes: CRITICAL PARSING: Exelthate (V-2AAM-2P) = aorist active imperative, 2nd person plural of exerchomai — decisive, urgent command. Ho laos mou = nominative-for-vocative (article T-NSM + noun N-NSM in direct address). Two hina-me clauses: (1) hina me synkoinonesete (V-AAS-2P, aorist subjunctive of synkoinoneo G4790) tais hamartiais autes — 'lest you co-participate in her sins'; (2) hina me labete (V-2AAS-2P, aorist subjunctive of lambano G2983) — 'lest you receive of her plagues.' The aorist subjunctive in both views the action as a bounded potential event — they have NOT YET done it. Rev 18:5: ekollethesan (V-API-3P, aorist passive of kollao G2853) — sins 'were glued together' reaching heaven. Emnemoneussen (V-AAI-3S of mnemoneuo G3421) — God remembered.

verses.heading: Judgment specification following the come-out call — double recompense and 'in one hour'
verses.kjv_text: Reward her even as she rewarded you, and double unto her double according to her works: in the cup which she hath filled fill to her double. How much she hath glorified herself, and lived deliciously, so much torment and sorrow give her: for she saith in her heart, I sit a queen, and am no widow, and shall see no sorrow. Therefore shall her plagues come in one day, death, and mourning, and famine; and she shall be utterly burned with fire: for strong [is] the Lord God who judgeth her.
verses.context: The come-out call is immediately followed by the judgment decree. The double-for-double recompense pattern echoes the lex talionis. The 'in one day/hour' refrain (18:8,10,17,19) emphasizes sudden destruction. Babylon's self-delusion ('I sit a queen, am no widow') parallels Isa 47:7-8.
verses.notes: The sequence is critical: come-out call (v.4) → reason (v.5, sins reached heaven) → judgment decree (vv.6-8). This confirms the fall-then-call-then-judgment pattern.

verses.heading: Vindication of holy ones — God avenges against Babylon
verses.kjv_text: Rejoice over her, [thou] heaven, and [ye] holy apostles and prophets; for God hath avenged you on her.
verses.context: Rev 18:20 uses mixed vocative construction: ourane (true vocative) + hoi hagioi kai hoi apostoloi kai hoi prophetai (articular nominative-for-vocative). This answers the martyrs' 'how long?' from Rev 6:10.
verses.notes: The nominative-for-vocative in the address to saints, apostles, and prophets matches the grammar reference entry pattern (Rev 18:20 is explicitly cited there).

verses.heading: Blood of prophets and saints — ultimate reason for the come-out call
verses.kjv_text: And in her was found the blood of prophets, and of saints, and of all that were slain upon the earth.
verses.context: The chapter's climactic indictment: Babylon is guilty of the blood of the righteous. This provides the final theological reason for the come-out call — the system is not merely wrong but murderous.
verses.notes: Connects to Rev 17:6 (drunk with the blood of saints) and the SP037 altar vindication arc (Rev 6:10 → 18:20,24).

verses.heading: OT come-out command — 'Go ye forth of Babylon, flee ye from the Chaldeans'
verses.kjv_text: Go ye forth of Babylon, flee ye from the Chaldeans, with a voice of singing declare ye, tell this, utter it [even] to the end of the earth; say ye, The LORD hath redeemed his servant Jacob.
verses.context: Isaiah's command to leave literal Babylon after the Persian conquest. The tone is joyful — 'with a voice of singing.' The exodus from Babylon is framed as redemption.
verses.notes: The verb is yatsa (H3318) in imperative. The command pairs departure with proclamation — those who leave are to DECLARE what God has done.

verses.heading: OT come-out command — doubled 'depart' with touch-not prohibition
verses.kjv_text: Depart ye, depart ye, go ye out from thence, touch no unclean [thing]; go ye out of the midst of her; be ye clean, that bear the vessels of the LORD.
verses.context: The second Isaianic come-out command, immediately following the gospel announcement of 52:7-10. Doubled imperative (suru suru) intensifies the urgency. The 'touch no unclean thing' prohibition is quoted by Paul in 2 Cor 6:17.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: — turn aside, DOUBLED) | צְאוּ (H3318 Verb.Qal.Impv.2mp — go out, appears TWICE) | מִשָּׁם (H4480+H8033 — from there) | טָמֵא (H2930 Adj.ms.Abs — unclean) | אַל תִּגָּעוּ (H408+H5060 Neg + Verb.Qal.Impf.2mp — do not touch) | מִתֹּוכָהּ (H4480+H8432+3fs — from the midst of her) | הִבָּרוּ (H1305 Verb.Niphal.Impv.2mp — purify yourselves) | נֹשְׂאֵי כְּלֵי יְהוָה (H5375 Verb.Qal.Ptcp + H3627 Noun.mp.Cst + H3068 — bearers of the vessels of YHWH)

verses.notes: HEBREW PARSING: suru suru (H5493, Qal Impv 2mp DOUBLED — 'turn aside, turn aside'), tseu (H3318, Qal Impv 2mp — 'go out') appears TWICE, tame (H2930 — 'unclean'), al-tiggau (H5060, Qal Impf 2mp — 'do not touch'), mittokah (H8432 — 'from the midst of her'), hibbaru (H1305, Niphal Impv 2mp — 'purify yourselves'). The LXX renders tseu with exerchomai (G1831) — the same root as exelthate in Rev 18:4 and 2 Cor 6:17.

verses.heading: OT come-out command — 'Remove out of the midst of Babylon'
verses.kjv_text: Remove out of the midst of Babylon, and go forth out of the land of the Chaldeans, and be as the he goats before the flocks.
verses.context: Jeremiah's first come-out command in the oracle against Babylon (Jer 50-51). The 'he goats before the flocks' imagery suggests leading the way out.
verses.notes: Part of the 12 verbal parallels between Jer 50-51 and Rev 17-18 established in 3am-12.

verses.heading: OT come-out command — 'Flee out of the midst of Babylon, deliver every man his soul'
verses.kjv_text: Flee out of the midst of Babylon, and deliver every man his soul: be not cut off in her iniquity; for this [is] the time of the LORD'S vengeance; he will render unto her a recompence.
verses.context: The urgency intensifies: 'flee' (nus, H5127). The reason is explicit: 'be not cut off in her iniquity' — staying means sharing her punishment. 'The time of the LORD's vengeance' parallels Rev 18:8.
verses.notes: The warning 'be not cut off in her iniquity' parallels the first hina-me clause of Rev 18:4 ('lest you share her sins'). The vengeance/recompense language parallels Rev 18:6.

verses.heading: Closest OT parallel to Rev 18:4 — 'My people, go ye out of the midst of her'
verses.kjv_text: My people, go ye out of the midst of her, and deliver ye every man his soul from the fierce anger of the LORD.
verses.context: The closest verbal parallel to Rev 18:4. Contains both 'my people' (ammi, H5971+1cs) and 'go out of the midst of her' (tseu mittokah). The reason is 'the fierce anger of the LORD' — paralleling 'her plagues' in Rev 18:4.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: — go out) | מִתֹּוכָהּ (H4480+H8432+3fs — from the midst of her) | עַמִּי (H5971+1cs — my people) | וּמַלְּטוּ (H4422 Verb.Piel.Impv.2mp — escape/deliver) | אִישׁ (H376 Noun.ms.Abs — man/each) | אֶת נַפְשׁוֹ (H853+H5315+3ms — his soul) | מֵחֲרוֹן (H4480+H2740 — from the burning/fierce) | אַף יְהוָה (H639+H3068 — anger of YHWH)

verses.notes: HEBREW PARSING: tseu (H3318, Qal Impv 2mp — 'go out'), mittokah (H8432+3fs — 'from her midst'), ammi (H5971+1cs — 'my people'), umaltu (H4422, Piel Impv 2mp — 'escape/deliver'), ish et-nafsho ('each man his soul'), mekharon af-YHWH ('from the fierce anger of YHWH'). This is the text Rev 18:4 most directly alludes to.

verses.heading: Post-exilic come-out call — 'Flee from the land of the north'
verses.kjv_text: Ho, ho, [come forth], and flee from the land of the north, saith the LORD: for I have spread you abroad as the four winds of the heaven, saith the LORD. Deliver thyself, O Zion, that dwellest [with] the daughter of Babylon.
verses.context: Post-exilic prophet still calling Jews to leave Babylon, showing many remained even after the Persian conquest. 'Dwellest with the daughter of Babylon' — covenant people inside Babylon by circumstance.
verses.notes: Significant: even after Babylon's political fall, God's people remained inside and needed calling out. This supports the progressive-fall reading — the come-out call continues after the initial fall announcement.

verses.heading: Abraham's original come-out call — proto-Babylon departure
verses.kjv_text: Now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house, unto a land that I will shew thee:
verses.context: The first 'come out' call in Scripture. Abraham is called from Ur of the Chaldeans — Chaldea being a synonym for Babylon. The call requires leaving country, kindred, and father's house — total separation.
verses.notes: Stephen confirms: 'The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia' (Acts 7:2). The LXX uses exerchomai. Heb 11:8: 'he went out, not knowing whither he went.' Gal 3:8 connects Abraham's call to the everlasting gospel.

verses.heading: Lot's come-out from Sodom — urgency and shared destruction
verses.kjv_text: And the men said unto Lot, Hast thou here any besides? son in law, and thy sons, and thy daughters, and whatsoever thou hast in the city, bring [them] out of this place: For we will destroy this place, because the cry of them is waxen great before the face of the LORD; and the LORD hath sent us to destroy it. And Lot went out, and spake unto his sons in law, which married his daughters, and said, Up, get you out of this place; for the LORD will destroy this city. But he seemed as one that mocked unto his sons in law. And when the morning arose, then the angels hastened Lot, saying, Arise, take thy wife, and thy two daughters, which are here; lest thou be consumed in the iniquity of the city. And while he lingered, the men laid hold upon his hand, and upon the hand of his wife, and upon the hand of his two daughters; the LORD being merciful unto him: and they brought him forth, and set him without the city. And it came to pass, when they had brought them forth abroad, that he said, Escape for thy life; look not behind thee, neither stay thou in all the plain; escape to the mountain, lest thou be consumed.
verses.context: Lot is a righteous man (2 Pet 2:7) inside a corrupt city. The angels compel departure. The warning 'lest thou be consumed in the iniquity of the city' directly parallels the first hina-me clause of Rev 18:4.
verses.notes: The 'cry' of Sodom 'waxen great before the LORD' (Gen 19:13) parallels 'her sins have reached unto heaven' (Rev 18:5). 2 Pet 2:6-7: 'delivered just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked.' Lot was righteous but proximity brought danger.

verses.heading: Korah rebellion — 'Depart from the tents of these wicked men, touch nothing'
verses.kjv_text: And he spake unto the congregation, saying, Depart, I pray you, from the tents of these wicked men, and touch nothing of theirs, lest ye be consumed in all their sins.
verses.context: Moses commands physical separation from Korah's faction before divine judgment falls. The 'touch nothing' prohibition echoes Isa 52:11 and 2 Cor 6:17. Proximity = shared destruction.
verses.notes: The pattern: (1) rebellion identified, (2) separation commanded, (3) those who remain are destroyed. Num 16:41-50 shows that even AFTER separation, 14,700 died from plague — proximity to rebellion brings consequences.

verses.heading: Lo-ammi / ammi reversal — 'not my people' becomes 'my people'
verses.kjv_text: Then said [God], Call his name Loammi: for ye [are] not my people, and I will not be your [God]. Yet the number of the children of Israel shall be as the sand of the sea, which cannot be measured nor numbered; and it shall come to pass, [that] in the place where it was said unto them, Ye [are] not my people, [there] it shall be said unto them, [Ye are] the sons of the living God.
verses.context: Hosea's children symbolize Israel's covenant status. Lo-ammi ('not my people') represents covenant revocation. But the reversal is promised: in the same place, 'not my people' will become 'sons of the living God.'
verses.notes: The lo-ammi/ammi reversal illuminates who ho laos mou are in Rev 18:4 — people who were formerly 'not my people' being reclaimed through the gospel call. Applied by Paul in Rom 9:25-26 and Peter in 1 Pet 2:10.

verses.heading: Hosea reversal fulfilled — 'I will say to them not my people, Thou art my people'
verses.kjv_text: And I will sow her unto me in the earth; and I will have mercy upon her that had not obtained mercy; and I will say to [them which were] not my people, Thou [art] my people; and they shall say, [Thou art] my God.
verses.context: The covenant restoration promise. God reverses the lo-ammi designation. The NT applies this to Gentile inclusion (Rom 9:25) and to the new covenant community (1 Pet 2:10).
verses.notes: The ammi (H5971+1cs, 'my people') designation is explicitly restored. This is the OT foundation for God calling people 'my people' who are currently outside the visible covenant community — exactly what Rev 18:4 does.

verses.heading: Paul quotes Hosea reversal — Gentile inclusion as 'my people'
verses.kjv_text: As he saith also in Osee, I will call them my people, which were not my people; and her beloved, which was not beloved. And it shall come to pass, [that] in the place where it was said unto them, Ye [are] not my people; there shall they be called the children of the living God.
verses.context: Paul applies the Hosea reversal to explain Gentile inclusion in God's people. The principle: God can designate people as 'my people' before they are visibly part of the covenant community.
verses.notes: kaleo (G2564) — 'I will CALL them my people.' The calling creates the identity. Applied to Rev 18:4: God CALLS people 'my people' while they are still inside Babylon.

verses.heading: Peter quotes Hosea — 'not a people' becomes 'the people of God'
verses.kjv_text: But ye [are] a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people; that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light: Which in time past [were] not a people, but [are] now the people of God: which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy.
verses.context: Peter applies the Hosea reversal to the new covenant community. The phrase 'called you out of darkness into his marvellous light' is itself a come-out formulation.
verses.notes: PARSING: laos (G2992) appears twice in v.10 — 'not a people' / 'people of God.' kalesantos (G2564, Aor Act Ptcp) = 'the one who called you OUT OF darkness INTO light.' The come-out call is embedded in the ecclesiology: God's people are CALLED OUT of one sphere INTO another.

verses.heading: True shepherd enters by the door — sheep hear his voice
verses.kjv_text: Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber. But he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep. To him the porter openeth; and the sheep hear his voice: and he calleth his own sheep by name, and leadeth them out. And when he putteth forth his own sheep, he goeth before them, and the sheep follow him: for they know his voice. And a stranger will they not follow, but will flee from him: for they know not the voice of strangers.
verses.context: The framework for understanding the come-out call as the shepherd's voice. The sheep HEAR and FOLLOW. The stranger's voice they will NOT follow. Recognition of the shepherd's voice is the prerequisite for leaving.
verses.notes: The phone (G5456, voice) of the shepherd is the mechanism of the come-out call. Rev 18:4 uses phonen ek tou ouranou — 'a voice from heaven.' John 10:3 describes the shepherd 'calling his own sheep by name and leading them out' — exerchomai again.

verses.heading: Other sheep not of this fold — aule vs poimne distinction
verses.kjv_text: And other sheep I have, which are not of this fold: them also I must bring, and they shall hear my voice; and there shall be one fold, [and] one shepherd.
verses.context: Jesus declares he has sheep OUTSIDE the Jewish institutional fold (aule). The dei (divine necessity, G1163) indicates this is God's mandate. The KJV 'one fold' mistranslates poimne (flock).
verses.notes: CRITICAL PARSING: aules (G833, N-GSF — fold/courtyard) ≠ poimne (G4167, N-NSF — flock). The sheep are not of this AULE (institutional fold) but will become one POIMNE (living flock) under one POIMEN (shepherd). Dei (G1163, V-PAI-3S) = divine necessity. The asyndetic construction 'mia poimne, heis poimen' (one flock, one shepherd) without conjunction emphasizes unified identity. This explains how God can have 'my people' in Babylon — they belong to his FLOCK even while in the wrong FOLD.

verses.heading: My sheep hear my voice — recognition as the basis of the come-out call
verses.kjv_text: My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me: And I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any [man] pluck them out of my hand.
verses.context: The sheep-shepherd relationship is defined by VOICE RECOGNITION and FOLLOWING. This is the mechanism by which the come-out call of Rev 18:4 functions — God's people inside Babylon HEAR the shepherd's voice and follow.
verses.notes: The ou me apoloontai (emphatic double negation — 'shall NEVER perish') provides security assurance for those who respond to the shepherd's voice. The come-out call is not abandonment but rescue.

verses.heading: First synkoinoneo parallel — 'Have no fellowship with unfruitful works of darkness'
verses.kjv_text: And have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, but rather reprove [them].
verses.context: Paul's prohibition against co-participation in 'works of darkness.' The verb synkoinoneite (G4790) is present imperative — an ongoing prohibition. Paired with elenchete (reprove) — exposure goes with separation.
verses.notes: PARSING: synkoinoneite (G4790, V-PAM-2P, Pres Act Impv) — present tense = ongoing prohibition ('keep not co-participating'). This contrasts with Rev 18:4's aorist subjunctive (potential single event). The 'unfruitful works of darkness' maps to Babylon's sins. The addition 'rather reprove them' shows separation is not passive withdrawal but active exposure.

verses.heading: Second synkoinoneo occurrence — positive sharing in affliction
verses.kjv_text: Notwithstanding ye have well done, that ye did communicate with my affliction.
verses.context: The Philippians synkoinonesantes (G4790, aorist participle) Paul's affliction — positive co-participation. This confirms the verb means ACTIVE participation, not mere proximity.
verses.notes: PARSING: synkoinonesantes (G4790, V-AAP-NPM, Aor Act Ptcp) — 'having shared in.' The positive use proves the verb means real, active participation. If synkoinoneo can describe genuine sharing in Paul's suffering, then synkoinonesete in Rev 18:4 describes genuine sharing in Babylon's sins — not mere geographical proximity.

verses.heading: Two-cup/two-table framework — participation requiring departure
verses.kjv_text: The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ? For we [being] many are one bread, [and] one body: for we are all partakers of that one bread. Behold Israel after the flesh: are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar? What say I then? that the idol is any thing, or that which is offered in sacrifice to idols is any thing? But [I say], that the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils, and not to God: and I would not that ye should have fellowship with devils. Ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord, and the cup of devils: ye cannot be partakers of the Lord's table, and of the table of devils.
verses.context: Paul's two-table framework: participation at one table excludes participation at the other. The cup of the Lord and the cup of demons are mutually exclusive. This provides the theological framework for understanding why the come-out call requires DEPARTURE, not merely mental disagreement.
verses.notes: PARSING (10:20): koinonous ton daimonion (G2844, N-APM — 'partakers of demons'). (10:21): ou dunasthe poterion Kyriou pinein kai poterion daimonion — 'you cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons.' metechein (G3348) = partake. Established in 3am-11: this is why the remedy is 'come OUT' rather than just 'think more clearly.'

verses.heading: NT composite OT catena — five antitheses and chiastic citation structure
verses.kjv_text: Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers: for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? and what communion hath light with darkness? And what concord hath Christ with Belial? or what part hath he that believeth with an infidel? And what agreement hath the temple of God with idols? for ye are the temple of the living God; as God hath said, I will dwell in them, and walk in [them]; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people. Wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean [thing]; and I will receive you, And will be a Father unto you, and ye shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty.
verses.context: Paul's most extensive 'come out' application. Heterozygeo (v.14, NT hapax from LXX Lev 19:19) frames the argument as species-mixing violation. Five antitheses (righteousness/unrighteousness, light/darkness, Christ/Belial, believer/infidel, temple/idols). Chiastic OT catena: A (v.16b) Lev 26:12/Ezek 37:27, B (v.17a) Isa 52:11, B' (v.17b) 'touch not,' A' (v.18) 2 Sam 7:14 modified.
verses.notes: PARSING (6:14): heterozygountes (G2086, V-PAP-NPM, Pres Act Ptcp — NT hapax, 'being unequally yoked'), metoche (G3352, N-NSF — partnership, NT hapax), koinonia (G2842, N-NSF). (6:17): exelthate (G1831, V-2AAM-2P — SAME FORM as Rev 18:4), aphoristhete (G873, V-APM-2P Aor Pass Impv — 'be separated'), akathartou (G169 — 'unclean'). The 'Lord Almighty' (pantokrator) closing occurs only here and in Revelation in the NT. Paul modifies 2 Sam 7:14: singular 'son' → plural 'sons and daughters.'

verses.heading: Original fall announcement — doubled epesen establishing fall-then-call sequence
verses.kjv_text: And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.
verses.context: The second angel's original message. The hoti clause gives the REASON: 'because she made all nations drink.' No demon-dwelling imagery yet (contrast 18:2). The doubled epesen is prophetic aorist.
verses.notes: The fall announcement (14:8) precedes the come-out call (18:4) in the narrative sequence. The hoti clause supports Bohr's claim that Babylon falls because she rejected the first angel's message — she 'made all nations drink' in response to the gospel call of 14:6-7.

verses.heading: Repeated fall announcement with demon-dwelling detail added
verses.kjv_text: And he cried mightily with a strong voice, saying, Babylon the great is fallen, is fallen, and is become the habitation of devils, and the hold of every foul spirit, and a cage of every unclean and hateful bird.
verses.context: The expanded repetition of 14:8. New content: katoiketerion daimonion (habitation of demons), phulake pantos pneumatos akathartou (prison of every unclean spirit), phulake pantos orneou akathartou kai memisemenou (cage of every unclean and hated bird).
verses.notes: Comparison with 14:8 confirms progressive deterioration. 14:8 has no demon-dwelling imagery; 18:2 adds three demonic descriptions. The egeneto (V-2ADI-3S, 'is become') marks transition — she was not always this way.

verses.heading: Transfer language — delivered from darkness, translated into the kingdom
verses.kjv_text: Who hath delivered us from the power of darkness, and hath translated [us] into the kingdom of his dear Son: In whom we have redemption through his blood, [even] the forgiveness of sins:
verses.context: NT 'transfer' language — the theological reality behind the come-out call. God delivers (errusato) from one domain and transfers (metestesen) into another. The come-out call is not just leaving but being relocated.
verses.notes: The come-out tradition is not merely negative (leaving corruption) but positive (entering the kingdom). Col 1:13 provides the NT theological framework for what happens when someone responds to Rev 18:4.

verses.heading: OT source for 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen'
verses.kjv_text: And, behold, here cometh a chariot of men, [with] a couple of horsemen. And he answered and said, Babylon is fallen, is fallen; and all the graven images of her gods he hath broken unto the ground.
verses.context: Isaiah's original 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen' — the source text for Rev 14:8 and 18:2. The doubled form in Isaiah announces historical Babylon's fall to Persia.
verses.notes: The LXX of Isa 21:9 uses peptoken peptoken (perfect of pipto) while Rev 14:8/18:2 use epesen epesen (aorist). The doubled form is preserved across Testaments.

verses.heading: God distinguishes his people from Egypt during plagues
verses.kjv_text: And I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms [of flies] shall be there; to the end thou mayest know that I [am] the LORD in the midst of the earth. And I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be.
verses.context: God puts a 'division' (peduth, H6304) between his people and the Egyptians during the plagues. This is the Exodus type of the come-out separation — God's people are protected FROM the plagues.
verses.notes: The pattern: God separates his people from the judged BEFORE the plagues fall. This directly parallels the second hina-me clause of Rev 18:4: 'lest you receive of her plagues.' The plagues of Rev 15-16 correspond to the Egyptian plagues in this typological framework.

verses.heading: Sodom's cry reaching God — parallel to Rev 18:5
verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous; I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto me; and if not, I will know.
verses.context: The cry of Sodom 'reaches' God, triggering the judgment sequence. This directly parallels Rev 18:5 where Babylon's sins 'have reached unto heaven.'
verses.notes: The reaching-heaven motif: Gen 18:20 (Sodom's cry reaches God → judgment), Gen 11:4 (Babel's tower reaches heaven → judgment), Ezra 9:6 (iniquities 'grown up unto the heavens'), Jer 51:9 ('her judgment reacheth unto heaven'), Rev 18:5 ('her sins have reached unto heaven'). The pattern: once sins cross this threshold, judgment is inevitable.

verses.heading: First angel's message -- the gospel call Babylon rejects
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: The first angel delivers the everlasting gospel. Babylon's fall (14:8) follows because she rejected this message.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Triggers the fall-then-call sequence: gospel call (14:6-7) -> fall announcement (14:8) -> come-out call (18:4).

verses.heading: Source text for 2 Cor 6:16b -- covenant dwelling formula
verses.kjv_text: And I will walk among you, and will be your God, and ye shall be my people.
verses.context: Levitical covenant promise quoted by Paul in 2 Cor 6:16. The A element of the chiastic catena.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Part of the 2 Cor 6:14-18 chiastic OT catena (A element).

verses.heading: Parallel source for 2 Cor 6:16b -- tabernacle/dwelling promise
verses.kjv_text: My tabernacle also shall be with them: yea, I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
verses.context: Ezekiel's parallel to Lev 26:12, also quoted in 2 Cor 6:16. Adds the tabernacle/dwelling motif.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Reinforces the A element of the catena with tabernacle imagery.

verses.heading: Source text for 2 Cor 6:18 -- Father-child language modified by Paul
verses.kjv_text: I will be his father, and he shall be my son. If he commit iniquity, I will chasten him with the rod of men, and with the stripes of the children of men:
verses.context: Davidic covenant promise. Paul quotes this in 2 Cor 6:18 but changes singular to plural, 'son' to 'sons and daughters.'
verses.notes: Supporting verse. A' element of the chiastic catena. Paul democratizes the Davidic promise.

verses.heading: Background for heterozygeo (2 Cor 6:14) -- species-mixing prohibition
verses.kjv_text: Ye shall keep my statutes. Thou shalt not let thy cattle gender with a diverse kind: thou shalt not sow thy field with mingled seed: neither shall a garment mingled of linen and woollen come upon thee.
verses.context: The Levitical species-mixing prohibition from which Paul derives heterozygeo in 2 Cor 6:14.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. LXX source for the NT hapax heterozygeo.

verses.heading: Israel's exodus from Egypt -- Pharaoh's forced come-out
verses.kjv_text: And he called for Moses and Aaron by night, and said, Rise up, [and] get you forth from among my people, both ye and the children of Israel; and go, serve the LORD, as ye have said. Also take your flocks and your herds, as ye have said, and be gone; and bless me also. And the Egyptians were urgent upon the people, that they might send them out of the land in haste; for they said, We [be] all dead [men].
verses.context: Pharaoh compels Israel's departure after the final plague. The Exodus is the foundational come-out event.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The Exodus pattern: plagues on oppressor, God's people separated and depart.

verses.heading: Seven last plagues -- what the come-out call prevents
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another sign in heaven, great and marvellous, seven angels having the seven last plagues; for in them is filled up the wrath of God.
verses.context: Introduction to the seven last plagues. The second hina-me clause of Rev 18:4 refers to these.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The plagues (plegon, G4127) that the come-out call aims to deliver God's people from.

verses.heading: Pouring out of plagues -- vials of God's wrath
verses.kjv_text: And I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth.
verses.context: The command to pour out the plagues. The come-out call (18:4) must precede this.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Sequential necessity: come-out call precedes plague-pouring.

verses.heading: Laodicean self-deception -- parallels blindness inside Babylon
verses.kjv_text: Because thou sayest, I am rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing; and knowest not that thou art wretched, and miserable, and poor, and blind, and naked:
verses.context: Laodicea's self-deception parallels the condition of those inside Babylon.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Babylon's wine disables perception of truth (pharmakeia, 3am-11).

verses.heading: Gospel preached to Abraham -- come-out call as gospel call
verses.kjv_text: And the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, [saying], In thee shall all nations be blessed.
verses.context: Paul connects the gospel to Abraham's call. Abraham's come-out (Gen 12:1) IS a gospel call.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Connects Abraham's come-out call to the everlasting gospel of Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Stephen recounts Abraham's call from Ur/Chaldea
verses.kjv_text: And he said, Men, brethren, and fathers, hearken; The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Charran, And said unto him, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and come into the land which I shall shew thee. Then came he out of the land of the Chaldaeans, and dwelt in Charran: and from thence, when his father was dead, he removed him into this land, wherein ye now dwell.
verses.context: Stephen confirms Gen 12:1 as a come-out paradigm. Abraham 'came out of the land of the Chaldaeans.'
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Confirms the Ur/Chaldea = proto-Babylon connection.

verses.heading: Abraham obeyed by faith -- 'he went out, not knowing whither he went'
verses.kjv_text: By faith Abraham, when he was called to go out into a place which he should after receive for an inheritance, obeyed; and he went out, not knowing whither he went.
verses.context: The faith dimension of the come-out call. Abraham obeyed without knowing the destination.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. 'Called to go out' (kalesantos + exelthein) links kaleo and exerchomai.

verses.heading: Faithful city become a harlot -- template for Babylon's identity
verses.kjv_text: How is the faithful city become an harlot! it was full of judgment; righteousness lodged in it; but now murderers.
verses.context: Isaiah's lament. Template for Babylon: once-faithful community now corrupt.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The zanah/porneia chain requires prior covenant relationship (3am-10).

verses.heading: God seeking his scattered sheep -- shepherd seeking flock everywhere
verses.kjv_text: For thus saith the Lord GOD; Behold, I, [even] I, will both search my sheep, and seek them out. As a shepherd seeketh out his flock in the day that he is among his sheep [that are] scattered; so will I seek out my sheep, and will deliver them out of all places where they have been scattered in the cloudy and dark day. And I will bring them out from the people, and gather them from the countries, and will bring them to their own land, and feed them upon the mountains of Israel by the rivers, and in all the inhabited places of the country. I will feed them in a good pasture, and upon the high mountains of Israel shall their fold be: there shall they lie in a good fold, and [in] a fat pasture shall they feed upon the mountains of Israel. I will feed my flock, and I will cause them to lie down, saith the Lord GOD. I will seek that which was lost, and bring again that which was driven away, and will bind up [that which was] broken, and will strengthen that which was sick: but I will destroy the fat and the strong; I will feed them with judgment.
verses.context: God as shepherd seeking scattered sheep. OT background for John 10:16.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. 'I will bring them out from the people' (v.13) = a come-out action by the shepherd.

verses.heading: Return, backsliding children -- God calls with shepherd promise
verses.kjv_text: Turn, O backsliding children, saith the LORD; for I am married unto you: and I will take you one of a city, and two of a family, and I will bring you to Zion: And I will give you pastors according to mine heart, which shall feed you with knowledge and understanding.
verses.context: God calls backsliding children to return. Promise of faithful shepherds.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The come-out call includes a positive destination: Zion with faithful shepherds.

verses.heading: Lost sheep parable -- shepherd seeks the one gone astray
verses.kjv_text: How think ye? if a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray, doth he not leave the ninety and nine, and goeth into the mountains, and seeketh that which is gone astray? And if so be that he find it, verily I say unto you, he rejoiceth more of that [sheep], than of the ninety and nine which went not astray.
verses.context: The shepherd seeks the one lost sheep. Parallels God seeking his people inside Babylon.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The shepherd actively seeks -- the come-out call is God's initiative, not human discovery.

verses.heading: Harlot identification -- the system God's people must leave
verses.kjv_text: And there came one of the seven angels which had the seven vials, and talked with me, saying unto me, Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore that sitteth upon many waters: With whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication, and the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication. So he carried me away in the spirit into the wilderness: and I saw a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns. And the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls, having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication: And upon her forehead [was] a name written, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH.
verses.context: Full identification of Babylon as the harlot system. Context for the come-out call.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. The harlot-bride bracket (Rev 17:1-3 / 21:9-10) established in 3am-10.

verses.heading: Plague after Korah -- proximity to rebellion brings destruction
verses.kjv_text: But on the morrow all the congregation of the children of Israel murmured against Moses and against Aaron, saying, Ye have killed the people of the LORD. And it came to pass, when the congregation was gathered against Moses and against Aaron, that they looked toward the tabernacle of the congregation: and, behold, the cloud covered it, and the glory of the LORD appeared. And Moses and Aaron came before the tabernacle of the congregation. And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying, Get you up from among this congregation, that I may consume them as in a moment. And they fell upon their faces. And Moses said unto Aaron, Take a censer, and put fire therein from off the altar, and put on incense, and go quickly unto the congregation, and make an atonement for them: for there is wrath gone out from the LORD; the plague is begun. And Aaron took as Moses commanded, and ran into the midst of the congregation; and, behold, the plague was begun among the people: and he put on incense, and made an atonement for the people. And he stood between the dead and the living; and the plague was stayed. Now they that died in the plague were fourteen thousand and seven hundred, beside them that died about the matter of Korah. And Aaron returned unto Moses unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation: and the plague was stayed.
verses.context: Aftermath of Korah's rebellion. Even after the initial separation (16:26), those who complained were struck by plague. 14,700 died.
verses.notes: Supporting verse. Reinforces the Num 16:26 come-out pattern.

word_studies.summary: From ek (out of) + erchomai (to come/go). The root verb of the exelthate command in Rev 18:4 and 2 Cor 6:17. 230 NT occurrences across 15 books. Primary LXX equivalent of H3318 yatsa (489 co-occurrences, PMI 4.42). Semantic category 'Separation and Covenant Departure' directly applies: God's people commanded to leave Babylon, unclean associations, or apostate communities. Key uses: Rev 18:4, 2 Cor 6:17, Heb 13:13, Acts 7:3.
word_studies.relevance: The verb of the come-out command. The aorist imperative form (exelthate) appears identically in Rev 18:4 and 2 Cor 6:17, creating a direct NT-to-NT verbal link. The LXX bridge to H3318 yatsa connects it to the entire OT come-out tradition.

word_studies.summary: A people viewed as a gathered or identifiable body. In NT usage often denotes Israel, the public crowd, or God's covenant people. 143 occurrences. Primary LXX equivalent of H5971 am (1,400 co-occurrences, PMI 26.79 — one of the strongest LXX bridges). Semantic categories: public crowd, Israel as covenant people, God's redeemed and priestly people, peoples among nations.
word_studies.relevance: Ho laos mou in Rev 18:4 is the covenant designation — God claims these people as HIS before they leave Babylon. The LXX bridge to H5971 am connects to the entire OT 'my people' tradition, especially the Hosea lo-ammi/ammi reversal.

word_studies.summary: Primitive root: to go out, come out, issue forth. 1,371 occurrences across 37 OT books. The 14th most frequent verb in the Hebrew Bible. Qal (784 occurrences) = intransitive 'go out'; Hiphil (279) = causative 'bring out.' The Hiphil Exodus formula ('brought you out of Egypt') is the theological backbone of the OT come-out tradition (~70+ occurrences). LXX primarily renders with exerchomai (G1831, 489 co-occurrences).
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew root of the entire OT come-out tradition. Every OT come-out passage uses yatsa: Gen 12:1 (Abraham), Isa 48:20, 52:11, Jer 50:8, 51:6, 51:45. The LXX bridge to exerchomai (G1831) connects directly to the NT exelthate command in Rev 18:4.

word_studies.summary: Compound of syn (together with) + koinoneo (to share/participate). Only 3 NT occurrences (or 5 tokens per Strong's DB): Eph 5:11 ('have no fellowship with unfruitful works of darkness' — present imperative, ongoing prohibition), Phil 4:14 ('ye did communicate with my affliction' — aorist participle, positive sharing), Rev 18:4 ('that ye be not partakers of her sins' — aorist subjunctive, potential co-participation). The syn- prefix intensifies koinoneo: not mere awareness or proximity but ACTIVE CO-PARTICIPATION. Bill Mounce: 'to share in company with, be connected with, be a joint partaker.'
word_studies.relevance: The specific verb chosen in Rev 18:4 to describe WHAT makes remaining in Babylon dangerous. The compound syn- prefix means the danger is not mere proximity but active co-participation. Phil 4:14's positive use confirms the verb means genuine sharing, not passive association.

word_studies.summary: NT hapax legomenon (only occurrence: 2 Cor 6:14). Compound of heteros (G2087, 'another of a different kind') + zygos ('yoke'). 'To be yoked with one of a different kind.' Derives from LXX Lev 19:19 where the prohibition is against species-mixing in cattle breeding. Paul frames the entire separation argument as a SPECIES-MIXING violation — the issue is ontological incompatibility, not mere preference.
word_studies.relevance: Paul's choice of this rare word from the Levitical species-mixing laws frames separation from Babylon as a matter of KIND, not degree. Light and darkness, Christ and Belial, temple and idols are different SPECIES — they cannot be yoked together.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun from an unused root meaning 'to associate.' A people as a congregated unit. 1,962 occurrences. The possessive ammi ('my people') is the primary covenant-identity marker (10% of all occurrences). LXX renders as laos (G2992) in 1,400 co-occurrences (PMI 3.7). Key formula: 'I will be their God and they shall be my people' — the covenant identity declaration.
semantic_categories: covenant exclusion (cut off from his people)
word_studies.relevance: The OT root of ho laos mou in Rev 18:4. The possessive ammi ('my people') in Jer 51:45 is the direct OT parallel. The Hosea lo-ammi/ammi reversal (Hos 1:9 → 2:23) shows the designation can be revoked and restored — illuminating who God's 'my people' are inside Babylon.

word_studies.summary: From plesso (to strike). A stroke, wound, calamity. 21 NT occurrences across 4 books. 14 of 21 in Revelation (3.5 per 100 verses). Translated 'plagues' 50%, 'stripes' 20%, 'wound' 20%. In Revelation, the word consistently refers to divine judgments — the seven last plagues of Rev 15-16.
word_studies.relevance: The second hina-me clause of Rev 18:4: 'lest you receive of her PLAGUES (plegon).' The plagues are the seven last plagues of Rev 15-16. The come-out call aims to separate God's people from Babylon BEFORE these plagues fall.

word_studies.summary: Of uncertain affinity. A shepherd (literally or figuratively). 18 tokens in 17 verses across 7 books. Translated 'shepherd' 94.1%, 'pastors' 5.9% (only Eph 4:11). John has highest concentration (5 uses). Used of literal shepherds (Luke 2), Christ as good shepherd (John 10:11,14), and church leaders (Eph 4:11).
word_studies.relevance: John 10:16's 'one shepherd' (heis poimen) is Christ. The come-out call is the shepherd's voice calling sheep from the wrong fold into the one flock. The asyndetic 'mia poimne, heis poimen' emphasizes that unity is in the shepherd, not the institutional fold.

word_studies.summary: From koinos (common, shared). To share with others. 8 NT occurrences. Semantic range: material sharing (Rom 12:13, Gal 6:6, Phil 4:15), sharing in suffering (1 Pet 4:13, Heb 2:14), complicity in wrong (1 Tim 5:22, 2 John 1:11). The base verb from which synkoinoneo (G4790) is formed with the syn- prefix.
word_studies.relevance: The base verb illuminates synkoinoneo. The 'complicity' sense (1 Tim 5:22, 2 John 1:11) shows koinoneo can describe unwanted participation in another's sin — the very thing Rev 18:4 warns against with the intensified compound form.

word_studies.summary: Alpha-privative + kathairo (to cleanse). Impure — ceremonially, morally, or demonically. 30 tokens across 8 books. 70% refer to unclean spirits/demonic beings. In Revelation: Rev 16:13 (unclean spirits) and Rev 18:2 (Babylon as habitation of unclean spirits). Paul uses for moral/ethical impurity (2 Cor 6:17 — 'touch not the unclean thing').
word_studies.relevance: Connects Rev 18:2 (Babylon as cage of unclean spirits) with 2 Cor 6:17 ('touch not the unclean thing') and Isa 52:11 ('touch no unclean thing'). The same vocabulary chain runs through the entire come-out tradition.

word_studies.summary: From hamartano (to miss the mark). A sin — the act, the resulting guilt, or (in Paul) sin as an enslaving power. ~173 NT occurrences. In Rev 18:4-5, the word appears in both the purpose clause ('lest you share her SINS') and the ground clause ('her SINS have reached unto heaven').
word_studies.relevance: Rev 18:4 uses hamartiais (dative plural) in the first purpose clause — 'lest you co-participate in her SINS.' Rev 18:5 uses hamartiai (nominative plural) — 'her SINS have reached unto heaven.' The sins of Babylon are a collective reality that those who remain inside become complicit in.

word_studies.summary: Primary verb: to cause to be, become, come into being, happen. 702 NT occurrences. The egeneto form (aorist middle indicative 3rd sg) is the classic narrative transition marker (233 of 668 forms). In Rev 18:2, egeneto marks Babylon's progressive deterioration: she 'IS BECOME' the habitation of demons — indicating she was not always this way.
word_studies.relevance: The egeneto in Rev 18:2 is a verb of TRANSITION — Babylon 'became' a demon habitation, implying progressive moral deterioration before the sudden judicial destruction. This supports the progressive-fall reading established in 3am-12.

word_studies.summary: Probably akin to phaos (light) through the idea of disclosure. A tone, voice, sound. 141 occurrences. Revelation has 34% of all occurrences (44 of 129, 10.9 per 100 verses). Primary LXX equivalent of H6963 qol (388 co-occurrences). Semantic categories include divine/heavenly voice, loud voice/exclamation, recognizable personal voice, and apocalyptic proclamation.
word_studies.relevance: Rev 18:4 introduces the come-out call as 'another voice from heaven' (allen phonen ek tou ouranou). The voice vocabulary connects to John 10:3,27 — the shepherd's voice that the sheep HEAR and FOLLOW. The come-out call is the shepherd's voice reaching his flock inside Babylon.

word_studies.summary: To fall. 93 occurrences across 10 books. Revelation has highest density (5.0 per 100 verses, 20 occurrences). Overwhelmingly aorist (80%). Semantic category 'Destruction of Cities and Powers' directly applies: Rev 14:8 and 18:2 both use the doubled epesen epesen for Babylon's fall.
word_studies.relevance: The doubled epesen epesen (prophetic aorist) in Rev 14:8 and 18:2 is the fall announcement that triggers the come-out call. Established in 3am-12: the prophetic aorist declares future event as accomplished fact.

word_studies.summary: Impersonal verb: it is necessary, there is need, one must. 104 unique verses. From deo (to bind): what is binding/necessary. Force ranges from absolute divine necessity to moral obligation. Dominant translation: 'must' (54.8%).
word_studies.relevance: In John 10:16, dei me agagein ('it is necessary for me to bring') expresses DIVINE NECESSITY — God MUST bring the other sheep. This is not optional or contingent but bound by divine purpose. The come-out call is backed by divine mandate.

word_studies.summary: From porneuo (to act as a harlot). Harlotry, fornication. 25 unique verses. Highest concentration in Revelation (7 occurrences, 1.7/100v). In Revelation, used figuratively for Babylon's corrupting spiritual influence. Strongest Hebrew bridge is the zanah word family (H2181).
word_studies.relevance: Babylon's defining sin. The zanah/porneia chain (established in 3am-10) requires prior covenant relationship — confirming the external corpus claim that Babylon targets 'religious bodies once pure and become corrupt' (GC 382).

word_studies.summary: From ek (out of) + derivative of kaleo (to call). A calling out — a popular meeting, religious congregation. 118 occurrences. Etymology itself IS a come-out concept: the church is the 'called-out ones.'
word_studies.relevance: The etymology (ek + kaleo = 'called out') shows the church's identity is inherently a come-out concept. The ekklesia IS the community that has responded to the come-out call. This connects the ecclesiological discussion of visible/invisible community to the Rev 18:4 call.

word_studies.summary: To call, properly aloud. 154 occurrences. Used for naming, inviting, and divine calling/election. In Rom 9:25, Paul uses kaleo to describe God 'calling' former not-my-people into his people — the Hosea reversal. In 1 Pet 2:9, kalesantos ('the one who called you') describes God calling out of darkness into light.
word_studies.relevance: The calling verb behind ekklesia. God CALLS people out — Rom 9:25 (calls them 'my people'), 1 Pet 2:9 ('called you out of darkness'), Heb 11:8 (Abraham 'when he was CALLED to go out'). The come-out call of Rev 18:4 is God's kaleo reaching his people inside Babylon.

word_studies.summary: From meta (after/with) + noeo (to think). To change one's mind, repent. 36 occurrences. Fundamentally cognitive and volitional — a deliberate reorientation, not merely emotional remorse.
word_studies.relevance: The response required after the come-out call. Rev 2:21 shows Babylon-type figures given 'space to repent' but refusing. The come-out call includes implicit repentance — leaving Babylon IS metanoia in action.

word_studies.summary: From koinonos (sharer). Common — shared by all (positive sense) or profane/defiled (ceremonial sense). 10 unique verses, 12 tokens. Dual meaning: what is common to all is, by definition, not set apart. Root of the koinon- word family including koinoneo, koinonia, synkoinoneo.
word_studies.relevance: The root concept behind synkoinoneo (G4790). What is 'common' (koinos) is the opposite of what is 'holy' (hagios). The come-out call moves God's people from the common/profane sphere (Babylon) into the holy/set-apart sphere.

parallels.connection_type: vocabulary + structural — closest OT verbal parallel: both have 'go out' + 'from midst of her' + 'my people' + reason of avoiding divine judgment
vocabulary — LXX uses exerchomai (G1831), same root as exelthate in Rev 18:4; 'touch no unclean thing' quoted in 2 Cor 6:17
vocabulary — identical verb form exelthate (V-2AAM-2P); both quote Isa 52:11; same come-out theology
structural — sins/cry 'reaching' God/heaven triggers judgment sequence in both
vocabulary — doubled 'is fallen, is fallen' in both; LXX peptoken vs NT epesen
theological — God seeking scattered sheep, gathering from every place; shepherd-seeking-flock motif
vocabulary + structural — 'my sheep hear my voice' (0.479 hybrid score); voice-recognition theme

Text: וְהָיָ֣ה בַּיֹּ֣ום הַה֗וּא אֶֽעֱנֶה֙ נְאֻם־יְהוָ֔ה אֶעֱנֶ֖ה אֶת־הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם וְהֵ֖ם יַעֲנ֥וּ אֶת־הָאָֽרֶץ׃

Note: This is the chain-of-response verse (heaven answers earth). The ammi reversal declaration is in the larger context: 'I will say to them which were not my people (lo-ammi), Thou art my people (ammi); and they shall say, Thou art my God.'
